title: Main antagonist
wikitext:
[]: Main antagonist...             Quote Post by GojiSeb » Tue Oct 08, 2013 6:05 pm Now, I know most will say that there are no true "villains" in NGE, but let's be reasonable here I think Keel Lorenz is an actual villain here. However, I've heard some identify Gendo as the primary antagonist. Who do you consider the main "villain" of NGE?

[]:             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Tue Oct 08, 2013 6:14 pm Shinji, himself. He's his own worst enemy.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Oct 08, 2013 6:44 pm If you mean in the usual sense it's Gendo. Nerv is a James Bond villain organization in a world with no good guys to oppose them. On every other level, every character in Eva has their own little story and in every story, they are their own worst enemy.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Tue Oct 08, 2013 7:05 pm I've actually heard an interesting case for how Yui is the primary antagonist. One could argue that all the events set in motion stemmed from her actions and her goals. That, however, is based upon how much agency one has to have in order to be an antagonist: if the primary cause of conflict was a result of her actions, but she is not actually the active agent in perpetuating it, does that make Yui an antagonist still? It's one of those fun little thoughts I like to roll around in my head just to hit the topic from a completely bizarre angle.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Oct 08, 2013 7:17 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: I've actually heard an interesting case for how Yui is the primary antagonist. One could argue that all the events set in motion stemmed from her actions and her goals.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Oct 08, 2013 7:24 pm That doesn't mean she's not an evil lunatic, only that she's an evil lunatic with different goals that exploited other evil lunatics. Who may not actually be evil.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Oct 08, 2013 7:33 pm Chuckman wrote: That doesn't mean she's not an evil lunatic, only that she's an evil lunatic with different goals that exploited other evil lunatics. Who may not actually be evil.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Tue Oct 08, 2013 7:51 pm Well, remember that an antagonist does not have to be evil to be an antagonist...

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Oct 08, 2013 8:19 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: Well, remember that an antagonist does not have to be evil to be an antagonist...

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Oct 08, 2013 8:23 pm Freaky : IMO, Yui waited until Eva-02 was down before she moved simply because she needed Eva-02 out of the picture in order to carry out her plan. "Nothing personal, but you and your pilot are hellbent on stopping HIP, the very thing I've been waiting to hijack." Gob Hobblin wrote: Well, remember that an antagonist does not have to be evil to be an antagonist...

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Oct 08, 2013 8:40 pm Reichu wrote: Freaky : IMO, Yui waited until Eva-02 was down before she moved simply because she needed Eva-02 out of the picture in order to carry out her plan. "Nothing personal, but you and your pilot are hellbent on stopping HIP, the very thing I've been waiting to hijack."

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Tue Oct 08, 2013 9:05 pm Personally, I do feel Seele was the more overt antagonist of the series, but I do like to play Devil's Advocate. In the case of Yui, one could argue that her agenda (which was simply a different twist on that of Gendo's, Seele's, and the Angels') definitely put her in opposition to Misato, who wanted to stop Instrumentality. Misato failed (which in itself has an interesting little subtwist: Misato and Yui as opposing mother figures in Shinji's life, the actual and the ideal), but in the end Shinji rejected Instrumentality altogether: this leaves the ambiguous note of who actually won, Yui or Misato (was Instrumentality given a way out?). Seeing as Yui still got what she wanted, which was to exist eternally as Eva as a monument to humanity, perhaps she got what she wanted in the end. I don't necessarily see her as an antagonist, since Yui's goals are so esoteric as to defy rational concepts of morality, but I can see why the case can be made.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Oct 08, 2013 9:50 pm Reichu wrote: I think it's mostly through "overanalysis" that people arrive at the conclusion that Yui must be evil. There isn't really anything implicit in the show to suggest this at all. At worst, she is morally ambiguous; but it would be the rare human struggle that did not involve moral ambiguities. Taking the show for what Anno seems to intend, Seele are unambiguously the "bad guys", as they have given up on humanity and want to drag all people down with them in their desire to run away to a form of primordial (non)existence. This makes Yui's intentions by default "good", or at least renders her plan the lesser of two evils. The audience may decide, independent of this, that they think Seele's idea is actually a good one, but this does not stop Seele from being the principal antagonists in a traditional sense. They are certainly not the protagonists...

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Oct 08, 2013 10:37 pm Chuckman wrote: What Yui did to her husband and son and the end result of her agenda are pretty unambiguously evil. Or insane, anyway. Her goal is actually less sane than Seele's.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Tue Oct 08, 2013 10:41 pm But the goal wasn't to stop Seele: it was to hijack Instrumentality, in order to achieve her own objectives of becoming an immortal monument. Whether Instrumentality succeeded or not had no bearing on Yui's plans.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Oct 08, 2013 10:54 pm She had the tools to stop Seele, or radically change the outcome, and picked "abandon my son whose life was ruined by the trauma of my 'death' and become a statue or something" instead. Her whole speech at the end of EoE is total bullshit: You can be happy anywhere. Enjoy waking up in hell next to the girl whose totally justified loathing of your spineless self-absorption led you to order humanity's execution. If you need me, I'll be in space where you'll never see me again!

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Oct 08, 2013 10:55 pm Chuckman : If you're going to be like that, there's no real point in having a conversation. I've learned to throw the Yui haters a bone by acknowledging that she isn't an infallible goddess, but the haters similarly need to acknowledge the "big picture". The idea that Yui could have stopped Seele early on, but didn't, and instead decided to screw her family over so she could selfishly deify herself is perhaps tempting in that humans like to have concrete villains, but kind of amounts to "wishful thinking" in the context of the actual show. As I noted, Yui is not presented as this kind of character; you could claim that Anno is intentionally trying to trick us, or perhaps, more simply, he doesn't consider her a villain and so did not give her villainous attributes. And exactly WHAT could Yui have done to stop Seele early on? If something were possible, don't you think Gendo and Fuyutsuki would have also thought of it? Her speech is "bullshit" if you twist its meaning to the most cynical lowest denominator, sure. Cynicism can be used to pervert just about anything. It becomes pretty tiresome that way. On another note, you seem to be far more angry about Yui's departure than Shinji was. Gob Hobblin wrote: But the goal wasn't to stop Seele: it was to hijack Instrumentality, in order to achieve her own objectives of becoming an immortal monument.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Tue Oct 08, 2013 11:00 pm Is the dissolution carried out by Eva-01? It was unclear at that point, but I was under the impression that it was Rei/Lilith that did that: wasn't she using 01 as a vessel at that point?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Oct 08, 2013 11:19 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: Is the dissolution carried out by Eva-01? It was unclear at that point, but I was under the impression that it was Rei/Lilith that did that: wasn't she using 01 as a vessel at that point?

[]:             Quote Post by peripateia » Tue Oct 08, 2013 11:32 pm I never took Eva to be a conventional good-vs-evil morality story, even though it is essentially a coming-of-age story and the two genres often mix. Antagonist-wise, I would say the enemy is deliberately uncertain, as Anno and Gainax intended. This is emphasized by the distorted, amorphous shapes and origins of the angels, all the up to Kaworu -who is shown not to be an enemy at all but Shinji's only true friend. At several points in NGE, Shinji expresses his uncertainty over who the enemy is (an uncertainty Asuka condemns and Rei ignores). I'd say the Children in NGE are fighting against a feeling of their era (ridiculous, I know-but bear with me) rather than against physical monsters or characters. This feeling of uncertainty for the future in the aftermath of an apocalypse, of insecurity in a constant state of combat-ready alertness, pushes the pilots to ask painful questions of themselves: where they came from, what drives their efforts, what they want from themselves and from others. The process is painful enough to drive Asuka to attempt suicide. I argue that Shinji learns, on that beach in EoE, to live with this hateful uncertainty despite its discomfort. I see instrumentality as the ultimate source of certainty and comfort, which comes at great cost to the expression of self, akin to death. And of course, Anno tortures us by ending EoE on such an uncertain note. Having a clear-cut enemy would make life simpler, but by coming to terms with what you fear (your perceived enemies), you become acquainted with yourself and learn slowly to accept yourself. That's the message I got from Eva

 alertness, pushes the pilots to ask painful questions of themselves: where they came from, what drives their efforts, what they want from themselves and from others.
